title: John Gaeta's Escape.ai Launches on Roku, Fire TV, Samsung and LG TVs
text:
Feb 3, 2026 5:30am PT
John Gaeta’s Escape.ai Launches Streaming Apps for ‘Prestige’ AI-Generated Content Across Roku, Fire TV, Samsung and LG TVs
Start-up founded by VFX veteran inks connected-TV distribution deal with Play.Works
By
Todd Spangler
Plus Icon
Todd Spangler
NY Digital Editor
xpangler
Latest

36 minutes ago

22 hours ago

Courtesy of escape.ai
Wait, can there be such a thing as “prestige” AI content?
John Gaeta
, the VFX veteran who won a visual-effects Oscar for “The Matrix,” believes there’s a potentially huge audience for curated, high-quality films and series created with the help of generative AI technologies.
Call it an anti-slop play: Gaeta’s
escape.ai
has built a streaming service dedicated to monetizing next-generation entertainment and interactive experiences built with AI, video-game engines and other modern production tools. “This is the prestige destination for cutting-edge generative AI content, but that’s not what we call it — we call it ‘Neo Cinema,'” says Gaeta, in a seeming nod to the hero of “The Matrix” movies.
Related Stories
Wild Bunch Takes Hit Survival Thriller ‘Balandrau' (EXCLUSIVE)
'Codename: Annika' Director AJ Annila to Helm Finland-U.K. Eco-Thriller 'Sands of Sarasvati' (EXCLUSIVE)
Now, after
launching on the web a year ago
, escape.ai is coming to connected TVs. Through a distribution deal with Play.Works, a provider of CTV games and video, apps for “Escape: Neo Cinema and Beyond” are available on Roku and the Roku Channel, Amazon Fire TV, LG and Samsung smart TVs. All together, the four platforms represent more than 400 million active devices worldwide. An escape.ai streaming app for Apple TV is next in the queue, Gaeta says.
Popular on Variety
Escape.ai is stocked with more than 1,000 original creator-led projects, spanning sci-fi, fantasy, anime, comedy and horror. The service features collections such as GenAIme (anime-inspired), Brave New Worlds (futuristic and dystopian) and Game Worlds (next-gen game concepts).
“Escape.ai is not just a channel — it’s an entirely new frontier for cinema,” Gaeta claims. “By partnering with Play.Works, we’re bringing creators and audiences together in a revolutionary new entertainment space built for the connected generation, instantly available in the living room.”
“You won’t see the word ‘AI’ on our home screen,” Gaeta adds. “It’s less to do with AI and more to do with creator empowerment, to have a new option to distribute works in a prestigious way and get exposure.”
Tel Aviv-based Play.Works says it distributes more than 400 game titles on connected-TV platforms, including Pac-Man, SpongeBob Bounce, Wheel of Fortune and Tetris. It also operates streaming channels like Jordan and Salish Matter, The Royalty Family and Ninja Kidz TV.
“Escape.ai represents the bold future of storytelling — inventive, interactive, and designed for how Gen Z experiences entertainment,” said Jonathan Boltax, CEO of Play.Works. “We’re thrilled to bring this visionary content to life across Roku, Amazon Fire TV, and Samsung, with more platforms coming soon — reaching audiences where they live, play and discover new worlds.”
Content on escape.ai is mostly free today. But the business model is to shift to premium subscriptions once it hits critical mass, Gaeta says. The company also is selling ads and sponsorships, and it’s offering creators the ability to rent or sell their content on escape.ai or accept contributions from viewers. (Participating creators aren’t required to exclusively put their projects on the service.)
Today, escape.ai receives hundreds of submissions each week, most of which a generally 5- to 20-minute films, according to Gaeta. “We review everything,” he says. “We have relationships with all of our creators.”
Gaeta, who is based in the San Francisco Bay Area, says escape.ai has fewer than a dozen full-time employees. He’s the majority owner of the company; he has some angel investors but won’t say who those are. Over the years, he’s worked for a range of companies including Lucasfilm, Epic Games and Magic Leap. More than two decades ago, Gaeta was the VFX supervisor for the Wachowskis’ Matrix movie trilogy.
A project Gaeta was himself involved with is on escape.ai: “Metropolis,” a short film directed by Aze Alter, with Gaeta and Kiri Hart, former SVP of development at Lucasfilm, serving as producers. It’s based on the manga title of the same name by legendary Japanese artist Osamu Tezuka (which alludes to, but is not technically an adaptation of, the Fritz Lang film).
Can escape.ai achieve liftoff in the crowded streaming market? Gaeta thinks his company can carve out a vibrant niche. “I would never think we could compete with Netflix and YouTube for quite some time,” he says. “But we’re more agile.”
